title: An efficient framework for quantum dynamics driven by nonclassical light
authors: Sheng-Wen Li, Zeyang Liao, Mao-Xin Liu
abstract: Understanding quantum system dynamics driven by nonclassical light pulses is challenging, particularly for general light states with large photon numbers. Here we introduce an efficient framework that makes this task tractable. By introducing a pulse-shaped P-representation, the exact quantum evolution is decomposed into a mixture of many independent quasi-classical branches, each governed by a standard master equation with a classical pulse which can be solved efficiently. As an illustration, for a two-level system interacting with an exponential pulse, we first find out the exact analytical solutions to the Bloch equations in each quasi-classical branch, and then by taking proper P-function average over all branches, the full system dynamics driven by nonclassical light pulses is analytically obtained. For the one-photon and two-photon cases, our method well reproduces the previous exact results either analytically or numerically. Crucially, our approach scales efficiently to more complex light states (Fock, thermal, squeezed vacuum states) with large photon numbers ($N\sim 100$). We further provide a clear physical interpretation how the system dynamics is influenced through the high-order optical coherence of the nonclassical pulses. This work provides a unified and computationally efficient route and a useful starting point to explore more complex quantum dynamics driven by nonclassical light in quantum optics and quantum information processing.
summary: This paper presents a new theoretical framework for calculating the quantum dynamics of a system driven by nonclassical light pulses. By using a pulse-shaped P-representation, the authors decompose complex quantum evolution into a manageable mixture of quasi-classical branches. This method allows for efficient simulation of states like Fock, thermal, and squeezed vacuum even with large photon numbers. The work demonstrates that the dynamics are heavily influenced by high-order optical coherence, which can suppress oscillations seen in classical driving.
main_problem: Modeling the quantum dynamics of a system driven by nonclassical light pulses is computationally intractable for large photon numbers using standard methods.
main_result: The authors developed an efficient framework that scales to large photon numbers (N ~ 100) by decomposing nonclassical evolution into a mixture of quasi-classical branches, successfully reproducing exact results for Fock and squeezed states.
method: A pulse-shaped P-representation is introduced to decompose the full quantum evolution into independent quasi-classical branches, each governed by a standard master equation, followed by an averaging process over the Glauber-Sudarshan P-function.
model_or_system: A two-level system (TLS) interacting with a 1D electromagnetic field and multi-mode nonclassical light pulses (including Fock, thermal, and squeezed vacuum states) under the Born-Markov approximation.
key_observables: Excited state population (excitation probability), Bloch vector components (u, v, w), and higher-order optical coherence functions.
important_parameters: Photon number (N), pulse linewidth (kappa), TLS decay rate (gamma), and single-photon coupling strength (eta_0).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes the electromagnetic field can be described by a fully quantized theory and utilizes the Born-Markov approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares excitation probability for various Fock states against coherent states, showing convergence at large N. Figure 3 illustrates the suppression of Rabi oscillations in thermal and squeezed vacuum pulses and shows their respective photon statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the pulse-shaped P-representation framework, validates it against known single- and two-photon results, demonstrates scalability to large N, and provides physical insights into the role of high-order coherence.
why_interesting: It provides a computationally efficient, unified route to explore complex quantum dynamics in quantum optics and waveguide QED, specifically bridging the gap between classical pulse treatments and fully quantum-mechanical descriptions for high-photon-number nonclassical states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Demonstration for a TLS interacting with a classical light pulse described by a wave packet ⃗E(r, t).

FIG. 2. The excitation probability evolution ¯Ne(t) from different in- put pulse states with total photon number N. The dot-dashed green lines show the situation when the input pulse is the coherent state |α, {βk}⟩with |α|2 = N as the total average photon number [from Eq. (15)]. The solid blue lines show the situation when the input pulse is the Fock state |N, {βk}⟩and are calculated by Eq. (25). The dashed red line in (b) also shows the situation of Fock input for comparison, which is calculated by the generalized master equation method [42–46]. The pulse shape {βk} takes the Lorentzian distri- bution (9), and here we consider γ = κ.

FIG. 3. The excitation probability evolution ¯Ne(t) driven by the ther- mal or squeezed pulses (γ = κ). The dot-dashed green lines in (a, b, d, e): the input light is the coherent pulse for comparison. The solid red lines in (a, b): the input light is the thermal pulse (28). The dashed purple lines in (d, e): the input pulse follows the photon statis- tics of the squeezed vacuum state (31). (c, f) Demonstration for the photon statistics {pn} for the thermal state and the squeezed vacuum state (mean photon number N = 10, corresponding to a squeezing strength r ≃1.87). The conditions are the same with Fig. 2.
